title: Nesting Controls Phase Transitions in Higher-Order Contagion
authors: Hugo P. Maia, Guilherme Ferraz de Arruda, Silvio C. Ferreira, Yamir Moreno
abstract: The organization of higher-order interactions plays a central role in shaping collective dynamics, yet a general structural principle governing contagion on hypergraphs remains lacking. Here we introduce a nesting coefficient that quantifies how lower-order interactions are embedded within higher-order ones, defining a continuum between simplicial complexes and random hypergraphs. Using a higher-order susceptible-infected-susceptible model, we show that increasing nesting lowers the activation threshold and suppresses discontinuous transitions, while weak embedding favors explosive behavior. We further demonstrate that correlations between nesting and interaction order modulate the onset of activity while only weakly affecting transition discontinuity. Analysis of synthetic and empirical networks reveals that nesting strongly predicts hysteresis, establishing it as a key structural determinant of phase transitions in higher-order systems.
summary: This paper identifies 'nesting'—the degree to which lower-order interactions are embedded within higher-order ones—as a fundamental structural determinant of phase transitions in hypergraphs. The authors show that high nesting stabilizes continuous transitions and lowers infection thresholds, whereas low nesting leads to explosive, discontinuous spreading and hysteresis. By testing this on both synthetic and real-world networks, they establish a predictive link between network architecture and collective dynamics. This provides a new framework for understanding how the organization of group-based interactions governs large-scale transitions.
main_problem: The lack of a general structural principle to govern how the organization of higher-order interactions (nesting) influences contagion dynamics and phase transitions on hypergraphs.
main_result: Increasing the nesting coefficient (the embedding of lower-order interactions within higher-order ones) lowers the activation threshold and promotes continuous phase transitions, while low nesting favors explosive, discontinuous behavior and hysteresis.
method: The authors introduce a nesting coefficient and use a bipartite configuration model with a node-exchange rewiring mechanism to create a continuum between simplicial complexes and random hypergraphs. They perform simulations on synthetic and empirical networks using a higher-order SIS model.
model_or_system: A higher-order Susceptible-Infected-Susipctible (hyper-SIS) model on hypergraphs and simplicial complexes, where infection occurs through $m$-order hyperedges and recovery follows standard SIS dynamics.
key_observables: Stationary epidemic prevalence (rho), activation threshold, transition type (continuous vs. discontinuous), hysteresis (bistability), and the nesting coefficient.
important_parameters: First-order spreading rate (beta 1), higher-order spreading rate (b), rewiring fraction (f), and the nesting coefficient (C).
main_assumptions: The bipartite configuration model assumes nodes can connect at most once, which may lead to frustrated configurations where connections cannot be completed.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the nesting coefficient and rewiring mechanism; Figure 2 shows prevalence plots demonstrating the transition from smooth to hysteretic curves; Figure 3 presents phase diagrams in the beta(1)-b plane; Figure 4 shows the effect of order correlations on dynamics; Figure 5 displays embedding patterns in empirical datasets; Figure 7 shows the exponential decay of nesting with rewiring; Figure 8 compares nesting across various real-world networks.
paper_structure: Introduction of the nesting problem, definition of the nesting coefficient and network generation methods, analysis of the role of nesting in phase transitions, investigation of correlations between embedding and interaction order, empirical validation using real-world datasets, and discussion of structural implications.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: (a) Example of the nesting coefficient of order 1 (pairwise) in a hyperedge h of order m = 3 (4-body), C(1) h . The coefficient represents the ratio between the number of 1st-order hyperedges and the maximum number of hyperedges that could be formed within the hyperedge of order m = 3. (b) Illustration of the rewiring mechanism used to control embedding. Two hyperedges of order m = 3 exchange one node (arrow), reducing the number of embedded pairwise interactions and yielding C(1) h = 2/3. Simulations are performed on networks with maximum order mmax = 5 (see also SM).

FIG. 2: Stationary epidemic prevalence ρ as a function of the first-order spreading rate β(1) for different rewiring levels f, corresponding to different average nesting coefficients ⟨C⟩. Forward (lower spinodal) and backward (upper spinodal) branches are obtained from initial conditions ρ(0) >∼1/N and ρ(0) <∼1, respectively.

FIG. 3: Phase diagrams of the higher-order SIS dynamics in the (β(1), b) parameter space for different levels of embedding (controlled by the rewiring parameter f and corresponding ⟨C⟩). Regions denote: I, absorbing phase (ρ = 0 globally stable); II, bistable regime; III, active phase (ρ > 0 globally stable). (a) Networks with only triadic interactions. (b) Networks with maximum order mmax = 5. Both systems have comparable average pairwise degree ⟨k(1)⟩≈8. Simulations are performed on synthetic networks with N = 104 nodes (see also the SM).

FIG. 4: Effect of order-dependent embedding on higher-order SIS dynamics. (a) Embedding patterns across interaction orders, shown for (i) negative, (ii) neutral, and (iii) positive correlations between embedding and order. Negative correlations correspond to stronger embedding at lower orders, while positive correlations correspond to stronger embedding at higher orders. (b) Stationary prevalence ρ as a function of the first-order spreading rate β(1) for the corresponding patterns in (a), showing that embedding concentrated at lower orders facilitates activation while having a limited impact on hysteresis. The higher-order infection rate is b = 0.34.

FIG. 5: Empirical evidence of order-dependent embedding and its dynamical impact. (a) Embedding patterns across interaction orders for representative datasets [35]. Top rows and right columns show the marginal averages over inner- and outer-order embedding, C(m) ↓ and C(M) ↑ , respectively (see also the SM). The overall embedding ⟨C⟩is indicated in each panel. (b) Scaled

FIG. 6: Example of the three steps employed to generate networks through the bipartite configuration model (see SM text). In this example, a bipartite graph is built with five nodes in partition A (circles) and four nodes in partition B (squares) following from predefined degree distributions P A k and P B k . Nodes from each partition can connect only once to each other, and nodes from partition B with the same degree cannot possess the same set of neighbors. The resulting graph is reinterpreted as a higher-order network with five nodes and four hyperedges.

FIG. 7: Overall average nesting coefficient ⟨C⟩as a function of rewires per hypergraphs f. Starting from a SC with ⟨C⟩= 1, the nesting coefficient drops exponentially as a function of the amount of rewires per hyperedge.

FIG. 8: Nesting coefficient measured from empirical datasets. Extra rows and columns on top and left represents the average inner and outer-order nesting coefficient respectively. Nesting coefficients for orders greater than 6 were omitted for better visibility.
